coredata.dc:title: In vivo imaging of neuroblastomas using GD2-targeting graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, You-Sheng
Chen, Yun
Tsai, Ya-Hui
Tseng, Sheng-Hong
Lin, Kuen-Song
coredata.dc:description:
Background Patients with neuroblastoma, a common childhood malignancy, often have poor prognosis. It is mandatory to develop an accurate and efficient diagnostic tool for neuroblastomas, so that the treatment can be started early. Graphene quantum dot (GQD), a nanomaterial, can be used to carry proteins, genetic materials, or drugs. GD2, a disialoganglioside, is a surface antigen expressed on neuroblastoma. This study investigated the in vivo targeting and imaging of neuroblastomas using GD2-targeting GQDs.
Methods GQDs were synthesized and conjugated with anti-GD2 antibody (anti-GD2/GQDs). In vitro cytotoxicity of GQDs and anti-GD2/GQDs was studied in human neuroblastoma cells by 3-[4,5-dimethylthiazole-2-yl]-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide)-based colorimetric assay. The tumor tracking and imaging of anti-GD2/GQDs in mice were investigated by in vivo imaging system (IVIS).
Results Treatment with GQDs or anti-GD2/GQDs induced no or mild cytotoxicity in fibroblasts and neuroblastoma cells. After co-incubation, GQDs and anti-GD2/GQDs were located in the cytoplasm and nucleus of neuroblastoma cells, with GQDs showing a blue fluorescence and anti-GD2/GQDs an orange/red emission. The IVIS images demonstrated accumulation of the fluorescence of anti-GD2/GQDs in the subcutaneous tumors in mice 24 h after intravenous injection of anti-GD2/GQDs.
Conclusions Anti-GD2/GQDs may potentially be used for the targeting and imaging of neuroblastomas in vivo.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
GD2
In vivo imaging
Nanomedicine
Neuroblastoma

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Surface modification of GQDs and bio-conjugations with anti-GD2 antibody
Detection of the fluorescence of anti-GD2/GQDs in mice
In vivo imaging of anti-GD2/GQDs in neuroblastoma xenograft model

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by research grant ( FEMH-YZU-2019-005 and FEMH-YZU-2020-004 from the Far Eastern Memorial Hospital ) awarded to Dr. Yun Chen and Dr. Kuen-Song Lin.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and size distribution of GQDs and anti-GD2/GQDs. (A & B) Representative TEM images of GQDs (average size: 4.15 nm) (A. 300,000 X, scale bar 10 nm; B. 8,000,000 X, scale bar 2 nm). (C) Dynamic light scattering histogram of GQDs. (D) Dynamic light scattering histogram of anti-GD2/GQDs.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectra and confocal micrographs of GQDs and anti-GD2/GQDs. (A) The PL spectra of GQDs. (B) The PL spectra of anti-GD2/GQDs. (C) Confocal micrographs of neuroblastoma cells transduced with GQDs (Excitation (Ex): 405 nm; Emission (Em): 420–480 nm). (D) Confocal micrographs of neuroblastoma cells transduced with anti-GD2/GQDs (Upper panel, Ex: 405 nm; Em: 420–480 nm; Lower panel, Ex: 514 nm; Em: 560–615 nm). The GQDs and anti-GD2/GQDs are localized in the cytoplasms and nuclei. Scale bar: 40 μm. BP: band pass. BF: bright field.

Cytotoxicity of GQDs and anti-GD2/GQDs. The NIH3T3 fibroblast cells or BE(2)-M17 neuroblastoma cells were co-incubated with various concentrations of GQDs or anti-GD2/GQDs, then the cellular viability was investigated by MTT assay. (A) The viability of NIH3T3 fibroblast cells 24 h after co-incubation with various concentrations of GQDs. (B) The viability of BE(2)-M17 cells 24 h after co-incubation with various concentrations of GQDs or anti-GD2/GQDs. (C) Microscopic images of neuroblastoma cells after 24 h treatment of PBS (Control), GQDs, and anti-GD2/GQDs.

In vivo imaging of GQDs and anti-GD2/GQDs in mice by IVIS. The neuroblastoma xenograft mice were injected with PBS (100 μL) or GQDs or anti-GD2/GQDs (0.4 mg particles in100 μL PBS) 24 h prior to imaging. The strongest fluorescence signal was revealed by 535 nm excitation and Cy5.5 emission. Representative images are shown. (A) Fluorescence signals in PBS-, GQDs- and anti-GD2/GQDs-injected xenograft mice. The tumor sites were indicated with the arrowheads in the thumbnails. (B) Parallel comparison of signal intensities of GQDs- and anti-GD2/GQDs-injected mice. (C) Fluorescence signal in the visceral organs and tumor from the anti-GD2/GQDs-injected mice.

head.ce:title: In vivo imaging of neuroblastomas using GD2-targeting graphene quantum dots

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors: Kuen-Song Lin, Department of Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Yuan Ze University, Chung-Li, Taoyuan, Taiwan, Tel.: +886 34638800 ext. 2574, FAX: +886 34559373; Sheng-Hong Tseng, Department of Surgery, National Taiwan University Hospital, 7 Chung-Shan South Road, Taipei 100, Taiwan, Tel: +886 2 23123456, ext.65110, Fax: +886 2 89665567.⁎ Equal correspondence.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors contributed equally for this study.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Patients with neuroblastoma, a common childhood malignancy, often have poor prognosis. It is mandatory to develop an accurate and efficient diagnostic tool for neuroblastomas, so that the treatment can be started early. Graphene quantum dot (GQD), a nanomaterial, can be used to carry proteins, genetic materials, or drugs. GD2, a disialoganglioside, is a surface antigen expressed on neuroblastoma. This study investigated the in vivo targeting and imaging of neuroblastomas using GD2-targeting GQDs.

GQDs were synthesized and conjugated with anti-GD2 antibody (anti-GD2/GQDs). In vitro cytotoxicity of GQDs and anti-GD2/GQDs was studied in human neuroblastoma cells by 3-[4,5-dimethylthiazole-2-yl]-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide)-based colorimetric assay. The tumor tracking and imaging of anti-GD2/GQDs in mice were investigated by in vivo imaging system (IVIS).

Treatment with GQDs or anti-GD2/GQDs induced no or mild cytotoxicity in fibroblasts and neuroblastoma cells. After co-incubation, GQDs and anti-GD2/GQDs were located in the cytoplasm and nucleus of neuroblastoma cells, with GQDs showing a blue fluorescence and anti-GD2/GQDs an orange/red emission. The IVIS images demonstrated accumulation of the fluorescence of anti-GD2/GQDs in the subcutaneous tumors in mice 24 h after intravenous injection of anti-GD2/GQDs.
Anti-GD2/GQDs may potentially be used for the targeting and imaging of neuroblastomas in vivo.

ce:section.ce:para: Neuroblastoma is a common childhood malignancy that arises from the neuroblasts within the adrenal medulla and peripheral sympathetic nervous system [1] . The prognosis of the patients with neuroblastomas is poor, especially in the high-risk group [1]. Early diagnosis and prompt treatment are helpful to improve the treatment outcome of neuroblastoma patients [1]. Currently, the diagnosis of neuroblastoma mainly depends on the clinical manifestations, laboratory findings, and imaging studies including ultrasonography, computed tomography scan, and magnetic resonance imaging. However, disseminating tumor cells and minimal residual disease may still escape the detection using these examinations. Therefore, it is mandatory to develop an accurate and efficient diagnostic tool, so that the treatment of neuroblastomas can be started early.

Nanomedicine, defined as the novel medical diagnosis and treatment tool with nanotechnology, has been applied to cancers for in vivo tracing of cancer cells and targeted drug delivery [2,3]. Graphene quantum dot (GQD) is a new-generation carrier for biomedical use as it has low cytotoxicity, high photo-stability, and excellent flexibility of functionalization in targeting and delivery [2,3]. Depending on the carbon precursors during synthesis, the synthesis method, and surface modification, GQDs can have a unique property of tunable emission activities by displaying different fluorescent colors [2,3]. Furthermore, bio-conjugating of GQDs with antibody has been reported to successfully target to and penetrate into cancer cells [2].

In this study, we synthesized GQDs, which were conjugated with antibody against GD2, a disialoganglioside and a surface antigen expressed on neuroblastoma, to become anti-GD2/GQDs. The efficiency of targeting and imaging of anti-GD2/GQDs were investigated in neuroblastoma cells. We found that there was significant accumulation of the fluorescence of anti-GD2/ GQDs in neuroblastoma cells both in vitro and in vivo.

The GQDs were synthesized through the pyrolysis method with citric acid as the starting material. Citric acid was put into a beaker and heated on a heating plate to 200 °C. The citric acid powder became aqueous solution and the color of the solution turned into orange in about 30 min. The obtained orange liquid was adjusted to pH 7 by NaOH solution. After that the solution was cooled to room temperature and the GQDs were purified by ethanol precipitation [4].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface modification of GQDs and bio-conjugations with anti-GD2 antibody
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs (7.8 gm) were dispersed in 50 mL sterilized water, and were mixed with polyethyleneimine (PEI) solution (1.5 gm) and polyethylene glycol (PEG) (1.5 gm) to enhance the bio-stability and facilitate the surface functionalization. The mixture was stirred for 60 min, and then autoclaved for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, the precipitated material was filtered out using a 0.22 μm filter. Then, the product was dried by a vacuum freeze drier for 24 h. Monoclonal anti-GD2 antibody was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). For the conjugation of antibody, N-hydroxysuccinimide/1-ethyl-3-(3- dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (NHS/EDC) method was used [2,5]. In brief, 200 µL of GD2 antibody (0.5 mg/mL) was dissolved in 200 µL of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) solution (0.5 M, pH 8.0). One hundred mg of cysteamine was dissolved in 1 mL EDTA-PBS (0.1 M) solution, which was then mixed with the antibody solution. After incubation at 37 °C for 90 min, the mixture was cleaned up with an Amicon cell (MWCO = 10 kDa). Two hundreds µg of Mal-PEG-COOH was added to the filtered mixture and incubated at 4 °C for 24 h. The mixture solution containing anti-GD2-functionalized PEG was filtered again with an Amicon cell (MWCO = 10 kDa). Ten µg of EDC and 10 µg of NHS were added to the purified solution and incubated at 4 °C for 10 min. One hundred µg of GQDs was then added, and the end solution was incubated overnight at 4 °C to form anti-GD2/GQDs.

NIH3T3 mouse fibroblast cell line and BE(2)-M17 human neuroblastoma cell line (American Type Culture Collection, Manassas, VA, USA) were used in this study. The cells were maintained in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), 2 mM L-glutamine, 1 IU/mL penicillin G, and 1 μg/mL streptomycin.

For this assay, 5 × 103 cells were seeded in triplicate wells of a flat-bottomed 24-well microtiter plate and cultured overnight prior to treatment. Cells were treated with various concentrations of GQDs or anti-GD2/GQDs for 24 h. The cellular viability was determined by the (3-[4,5-dimethylthiazole-2-yl]−2,5- diphenyltetrazolium bromide)-based colorimetric assay (MTT assay).

Confocal fluorescent images were obtained by a Zeiss LSM510 confocal microscopy with a 40x oil objective. The bright-field images and the fluorescence images were merged to confirm the localization of the fluorescence signals from the cells transduced with GQDs or anti-GD2/GQDs .

The animal experiment was approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of the Far Eastern Memorial Hospital, New Taipei, Taiwan, and conducted according to the guidelines of the Laboratory Animal Center of the FEMH (approval number: IACUC-2019(2)-FYZ-01 and IACUC-2020(2)-MOST-03). Four-week-old nude mice, weighing 15–20 gs, were used for the experiments. The mice were provided food and water ad libitum on a 12:12 h day-night cycle (lights on from 6:00 a.m. to 6:00 p.m.), with the room temperature maintained at approximately 20 °C. Subcutaneous tumor was induced by injecting 5 × 105 BE(2)-M17 cells (in 100 μl of PBS) into the right flank of each mouse. The health of the mice and the tumor growth were monitored twice a week. Until the tumor reached 200 mm3 in volume, PBS, GQDs or anti-GD2/GQDs (0.4 mg in 100 μl PBS) were injected into the tail vein of the mice. Twenty-four hours after injection, the fluorescence in mice was observed using in vivo imaging system (IVIS). The animal study was conducted in 32 mice, with 8 mice for the PBS control group and 12 mice each for GQDs-injected and anti-GD2/GQDs-injected groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of the fluorescence of anti-GD2/GQDs in mice
ce:section.ce:para: IVIS Spectrum Imaging System (PerkinElmer, Waltham, MA, USA) was used to detect the fluorescence in mice. Briefly, 24 h after injection of PBS, GQDs, or anti-GD2/GQDs, animals were anesthetized. Then the fluorescence imaging was performed using the following scanning condition: 535 nm excitation, Cyanine 5.5 (Cy5.5) (695–770 nm) emission. When the scanning was finished, the mice were sacrificed. The visceral organs and the tumor were harvested to run another fluorescence scanning.

All data were presented as mean values with respective standard deviations (SD). Differences between the study and control groups were assessed using Student's t-test, with P < 0.05 considered statistically significant.

GQDs were prepared under the hydrothermal process with citric acid as the carbon precursor. The product GQDs were scanned by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and the shape of GQDs was circular with high uniformity (Fig. 1 A & 1B). The diameter of the particle size was further analyzed by dynamic light scattering (DLS), and the average diameter of GQDs was 4.15 nm (Fig. 1C) and that of anti-GD2/GQDs was 156.98 nm (Fig. 1D).

To characterize and compare the fluorescence properties of GQDs and anti-GD2/GQDs, the particles were subjected to spectra screening of photoluminescence. For GQDs, a broad blue emission was observed between 400 and 450 nm (peak at around 440 nm) under the excitation of 340 nm (Fig. 2 A). For anti-GD2/GQDs, the particles had a sharp orange/red emission at around 525–575 nm (peat at around 535 nm) under the excitation of 500 nm (Fig. 2B). To further confirm the photoluminescence properties of the GQDs in neuroblastoma cells, cells transduced with GQDs or anti-GD2/GQDs were imaged by fluorescence confocal microscopy. Forty-eight hours after transduction, GQDs or anti-GD2/GQDs were noted to be co-localized in the cytoplasms and nuclei of the neuroblastoma cells (Fig. 2C & 2D). For GQDs, a strong blue emission between 420 and 480 nm (4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) channel) was observed under the excitation of 405 nm (Fig. 2C). On the other hand, anti-GD2/GQD particles showed lower intensity in the DAPI channel but shifted to an orange to red emission between 560 and 615 nm under the excitation of 514 nm (Fig. 2D). Collectively, the GQDs and anti-GD2/GQDs demonstrated distinct fluorescent expressions in neuroblastoma cells.

To examine the biocompatibility and the applicability of GQDs and anti-GD2/GQDs in biomedical use, the viability and morphology of the NIH3T3 fibroblast cells and BE(2)-M17 neuroblastoma cells were examined 24 h after the cells were transduced with GQDs or anti-GD2/GQDs (Fig. 3 ). The viability of the NIH3T3 and BE(2)-M17 cells transduced with GQDs showed no significant difference from that of the control cells (P < 0.05) (Fig. 3A & 3B). Transduction with anti-GD2/GQDs (0–20 μg/mL GQDs) did not affect the viability of BE(2)-M17 cells; however; higher concentration of anti-GD2/GQDs (40 μg/mL GQDs) reduced the mean cellular viability to 83.1% (P = 0.01) (Fig. 3B). Both GQDs and anti-GD2/GQDs did not affect cellular morphology (Fig. 3C). The results suggested GQDs and anti-GD2/GQDs induced limited cytotoxicity in both fibroblasts and neuroblastoma cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo imaging of anti-GD2/GQDs in neuroblastoma xenograft model
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the in vivo targeting activity of anti-GD2/GQDs for neuroblastoma cells, PBS, GQDs or anti-GD2/GQDs (0.4 mg/100 μl PBS) were injected into mice via the tail vein. Twenty-four hours after injection, the mice were scanned for fluorescence imaging by IVIS. With the use of 535 nm excitation and Cy5.5 emission, we observed concentrated fluorescence at the tumor sites in both GQDs- and anti-GD2/GQDs-injected groups; in contrast, there was no fluorescence detected in the PBS-injected group (Fig. 4 A). As compared in parallel, the signal intensity in the anti-GD2/GQDs group was significantly higher than that in the GQDs group (Fig. 4B). The tumors and visceral organs were then harvested and subjected to IVIS image, which showed high Cy5.5 emission mainly in the tumor. Only small amount of fluorescence were in the kidney, liver, spleen, and lung, with the kidney having more fluorescence than the other visceral organs. The results suggest anti-GD2/GQD particles mainly targeted to the neuroblastomas (Fig. 4C).

Nanomedicine has been applied to the diagnosis and treatment in cancers [6]. It can be used in fluorescence-guided surgery to provide a real-time, intra-operative observation of the cancer cells by injection of fluorophore-carrying nanoparticles [6]. In addition, as the nanoparticles can be equipped with tumor-associated targeting activity, the imaging agent-loaded nanoparticles can increase the specificity and accuracy of in vivo cancer imaging [6]. GQD, a nano-sized semiconductor with self-carrying photoluminescence, has also been applied to clinical field because it possesses some advantages such as low cost, high stability, and good bio-compatibility [2,3]. In this study, we synthesized GQDs and prepared anti-GD2/GQDs by conjugating GQDs to anti-GD2. Both GQDs and anit-GD2/GQDs were circular in shape. The size of GQDs was about 4 nm in diameter, which was similar to the literature [2,3] . However, the mean diameter of anti-GD2/GQDs was about 156 nm, which might be related to the aggregation of particles during or after the antibody conjugation process [7]. The cytotoxicity is an important factor to determine whether a nanomaterial can be used clinically. In our study, we found that 24 h treatment with GQDs showed no significant toxicity in the fibroblasts and neuroblastoma cells. The low toxicity of GQDs has also been observed in the MCF breast cancer cells, Hep-2 laryngeal cancer cells, A549 human lung epithelial cancer cells, and HT-29 human colorectal adenocarcinoma cells [8]. The toxicity risks of graphene-based materials have been found to be dependent on their surface functionalization and size [9]; however, we noted that anti-GD2/GQDs showed limited toxicity in neuroblastoma cells, although the diameter of anti-GD2/GQDs was larger than that of GQDs. From our data and the literature, GQDs and anti-GD2/GQDs have low toxicity; thus they potentially can be used for biomedical use. (2 use)

Another characteristic of GQDs is their tunable photoluminescence [9]. GQDs can display different emission spectra depending on the source material for particle synthesis, the process of synthesis, the size of particle, and the surface modification, etc. [9]. In our study, the GQDs made from citric acid showed strong blue emission, which is consistent with previous reports [3,10]. However, after surface functionalization of GQDs with PEG and PEI, there was redshift emission, that is, the longer emission wavelength of photoluminescence [11]. After conjugating GQDs with anti-GD2, the anti-GD2/GQDs showed a shift to an orange to red emission between 560 and 615 nm under the excitation of 514 nm. GQDs have also been applied in the cell imaging of fibroblast cells, stem cells, and various cancer cell lines, including tongue cancer cell, breast cancer cell, and lung cancer cells [8]. In these reports, GQDs were noted to efficiently penetrate the cell membrane and present stable fluorescence in the cytoplasm and/or nucleus in cell imaging [8]. Our study also found that GQDs and anti-GD2/GQDs were located in both the cytoplasms and nuclei of the neuroblastoma cells after co-incubation. The presence of intracellular fluorescence suggests anti-GD2/GQDs can attach the neuroblastoma cells through the surface GD2, then enter the cells in vitro, although anti-GD2/GQDs had larger size. We further performed animal experiment to investigate whether these nanoparticles can target to the neuroblastomas in vivo. Twenty-four hours after injection of anti-GD2/GQDs through tail vein, the orange to red fluorescence was accumulated in the tumor site of the mice and also in the tumors removed from the mice. Only small amount of fluorescence were in the visceral organs. The kidney showed more fluorescence than other visceral organs because GQDs are mainly excreted through the kidney. It was noted that injection of GQDs also led to the accumulation of fluorescence signals in the tumor site, although the fluorescence intensity was much lower than that induced by injection of anti-GD2/GQDs. The fluorescence induced by GQDs in tumor site might result from the enriched blood flow and hyper-permeability of the blood vessel in the tumor, which is correlated with the in vivo destination of nanomedicine [12]. These findings indicate that the anti-GD2 antibody-conjugated GQDs can target to the neuroblastomas in vivo. Bio-conjugation by tumor-targeting molecules using anti-Her2- and folic acid-conjugated GQDs had also been tested in breast cancer cells in vitro [2]. However, there is no report mentioning the use of anti-GD2/GQDs for the in vivo targeting and imaging of neuroblastomas.

Although our results revealed anti-GD2/GQDs can target to the tumors in mice, however, there are limitations in our study. The experiments in this study were performed in a neuroblastoma xenograft model in mice. The tumor size in mice was small, therefore, whether the nanoparticles could distribute to every part of a large tumor in large animals or humans needs further investigation. In addition, our animal model is a subcutaneous xenograft model, the in vivo experimental results could not be directly applied to the spontaneously occurring tumors in humans.

In this study, we demonstrated GQDs are highly potential nanomaterial for biomedical use, and anti-GD2/GQDs may be used for the targeting and imaging of neuroblastomas. As with high biocompatibility and great functional plasticity, GQDs hold great potential for clinical imaging. Such a nanomaterial may be developed into a diagnostic imaging tool and carrier of therapeutic agents for neuroblastomas, so that the tumor can be detected and/or treated at an early stage. Certainly more studies are necessary before any clinical application.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by research grant (FEMH-YZU-2019-005 and FEMH-YZU-2020-004 from the Far Eastern Memorial Hospital) awarded to Dr. Yun Chen and Dr. Kuen-Song Lin.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Matthay K.K., Maris J.M., Schleiermacher G., Nakagawara A., Mackall C.L., Diller L., et al. Neuroblastoma. Nat Rev Dis Primers2016;2:16078. https://doi.org/10.1038/nrdp.2016.78
